data.title: FLOW: An Underappreciated Alternative To COWZ
data.description:
Introduction

ETFs which measure stocks based on their free cash flow [FCF] characteristics, have been mushrooming in recent years; the Global X U.S. Cash Flow Kings 100 ETF (NYSEARCA:FLOW) is part of this bandwagon, having been listed only in July 2023. FLOW’s AUM of less than $15m suggests that it not very well-known in the broad retail community.

Importance of FCF and Screening Methodology

For those not familiar with free cash flow, it is a non-GAAP measure, which shows the idle cash that companies have left after making capital investments.

Businesses then typically deploy this free cash flow in three productive ways, all of which are designed to enhance value one way or the other.

Most businesses, particularly if they are still in the infancy of their lifecycle, will choose to reinvest the FCF back into the business or choose to use those funds to acquire other companies that could enhance their stature. Alternatively, businesses that are highly geared, could choose to use this cash to bring down their financial leverage. Besides these first two options, free cash flow could also be used to reward loyal shareholders by way of a dividend or the company could even choose to buy back its stock, particularly if management feels it is undervalued.

All in all, it’s fair to say that if a business can generate ample FCF year in, year out, it gives itself ample opportunities to build value, one way or the other. In that regard, wouldn’t it be great, if one could own a bunch of high FCF generating businesses in one basket, and also at a fairly reasonable cost?

Well, that’s essentially what FLOW sets out to do.

FLOW’s tracking index is the Global X U.S. Cash Flow Kings 100 Index. It first starts of by fishing in a broad market index called the Mirae Asset US 1000 Index, with financial services stocks weeded out; as noted previously, it doesn’t make a great deal of sense to gauge financial stocks by way of FCF. Besides financial services, even stocks with negative FCF Yield are cast aside (by FCF yield, we mean the last twelve months FCF as a function of the enterprise value of the stock)

Then the top 100 stocks by way of FCF yields are chosen with each stock’s individual weight capped at 2%.

How Good Is FLOW Relative To COWZ?

Before FLOW came to the bourses, another FCF based ETF- COWZ was already attracting a low of investor flows. Infact such is its popularity that its AUM amounts to over $20B, which is a whopping 1500x that of COWZ.

COWZ’s superior popularity is also validated by the degree of dollar volumes it sees every day; this matters because you end up getting tighter spreads and thus better fills on your position, especially if you like to make short-term bets. FLOW’s wide spreads between the bid and the ask of 0.27% are indeed quite concerning.

Like FLOW, COWZ too is a passively managed product that covers close to 100 stocks, but it tracks the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 Index. They key differentiating factor to note is that COWZ’s tracking index does not consider financial stocks or REITs, whereas FLOW considers the latter. Also while both products are mainly large-cap focussed products, FLOW deals with large-caps that are closer to the mid-cap barrier of $10B. Both products report almost similar tracking errors in covering their respective indices, with figures inching close to 10% (for context most passive ETFs report tracking errors of less than 8%).

Note that both products do well to keep concentration effects in play, with the top 10 stocks only accounting for a little over 20%. Frequent rebalancing means both these products see a high degree of churn every year, although FLOW has a higher turnover rate of 1100bps. This may not appeal to investor who seek stability in their portfolios.

In most other aspects, we believe the low-profile FLOW has an edge over COWZ.

Arguably the most important aspect-costs. FLOW is only priced at an expense ratio of 0.25%, which is roughly only half of what COWZ is priced at. FLOW also covers a bunch of stocks with cheaper valuations. On a weighted average basis, FLOW’s portfolio is priced at a P/E of single-digits, whereas COWZ’s holdings are priced at a 38% premium. Interestingly enough, despite being priced at a lower P/E, FLOW’s holdings are poised to deliver superior earnings going forward.

Hiterto, FLOW’s stock picking too has been far superior. One can see that in the magnitude of the outperformance since it got listed, be it on a price basis or a total return basis.

Since total returns also take into consideration the distribution, it’s worth noting that at current prices, FLOW also offers a superior yield exceeding 2% (COWZ’s yield is less than 1.6%).

Risk-conscious investors will also be interested to note that FLOW’s monthly returns are less variable. We find this quite intriguing given that COWZ tilts more to a defensive sector such as healthcare, whereas FLOW tilts more to a high-beta sector like consumer discretionary.

It also appears that FLOW has a better grip on the risk it takes to generate excess returns. This can be seen by the positive Sharpe ratio it has thrown up, whereas COWZ has seen a negative figure. The same goes for the Sortino ratio, which measures excess return capabilities during harmful volatility. Once again it is FLOW with the better risk-adjusted return track record.

Is FLOW ETF A Good Buy Now?

Even though FLOW has shown that it can adequately compete with its larger peer, and even trump it across various parameters, we feel an entry in this product at this juncture may not be too sensible.

As noted earlier, FLOW tilts more towards discretionary stocks, which are grappling with some headwinds. Firstly, it is questionable, if the US populace has the necessary appetite to engage in discretionary purchases. One can see that in the momentum of retail sales which appears to have gone off the rails, with May only witnessing YoY growth of 3.3%, the lowest in 7 months (for perspective, monthly retail sales on average have been growing at a pace of around 5% for the last 32 years.

This is validated even further by concerning readings on the consumer confidence front, which is more of a forward-looking indicator; what was particularly troubling was the “ expectations index” which sheds some insight into consumers’ expectations for income, business and labor market conditions, which dropped below a mark which usually signals a recession.

Faced with limited spending appetite from the target market, these consumer discretionary stocks run the risk of resorting to unhealthy promotions to stimulate volume, which ends up impacting profitability. In the upcoming Q2 results, don’t expect any fireworks from this sector, as it is poised to deliver the second weakest earnings outlook (after energy). Also note that around 3 months back, this sector was atleast looking at positive earnings growth of low single digits; now it is more likely to be negative growth of mid-single-digits!

In addition to all this, we are also not hugely enthused with the risk-reward on FLOW’s weekly chart.

Since it got listed, until the final month of last year, FLOW had been trending higher by bouncing off an ascending trendline; that trendline finally came to an end, and despite some faint efforts to recoup it, FLOW never quite managed it. After that we’ve seen strong weakness all through March. FLOW has since done very well to rebound from the March lows (although note that the volumes in recent weeks haven't been too strong in recent weeks), but now it is only around 4% off its lifetime highs, while a long way off its lows.

To conclude, in light of the unappealing risk-reward, and a discretionary sector tilt, we don’t think FLOW would make for a good buy now. Initiating with a HOLD.

data.title: Amazon's Robotics Revolution Has Quietly Arrived
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), the stock has gained 19% in price. This far exceeds the S&P 500's (SPY) 10.5%, indicating the alpha opportunity.

It's not surprising that Amazon is so far ahead of the macroeconomy when it comes to robotics. The greatest tech entrepreneurs are always usually iterating on the next major wave of growth about 10 years before everybody else. While the whole market is currently fawning of AI, Amazon and other major tech players were preparing a decade ago for the growth we're currently witnessing today. That's why it's not surprising that Amazon is already operational in robotics at scale—within 10 years, I won't be surprised if Amazon is starting to become predominantly operated by robotic processes. The margin implications of this will be very positive, but it also coincides with more nuanced macro discussions about sustainable demand amid the loss of general, non-specialized human labor. The antidote to this appears to be that humans will need to train in more advanced fields (i.e., for many industries, rote work will become a task of the past). In construction and culinary, as core examples, we may see robotic aides rather than complete replacement of human labor, but in a business like warehousing and delivery, robotics could take jobs almost entirely before we know it.

Short-Term Capex Sets the Stage for Long-Term Margin Dominance

In Fiscal 2024, Amazon spent a total of $77.7 billion on capex. Of this, it probably spent about $7-8 billion on robotics, given that most of the capex is spent on AWS and AI infrastructure. Probably $20-30 billion of total capex was spent on fulfillment networks. Of the fulfillment capex, an even smaller slice would be devoted to robotics. $7 billion is the best estimate I can conjur right now, and it is also supported by other industry reports.

This fulfillment-related robotics capex is almost certainly going to rise aggressively as the company seeks to consolidate its moat in autonomous warehousing and delivery. Amazon has to spend about $450 million per robotics fulfillment center, so such high spending is likely to strain near-term cash flows even though it significantly strengthens Amazon's long-term moat. These costs facilitate installation, software, and training and maintenance. There are also likely to be logistical constraints in the near term as Amazon's automated operations are iterated on and optimized for efficiency. Delays in executing a road map can be costly, especially if human labor is significantly reduced and manual workarounds become less feasible. However, management is taking the approach of gradually scaling into robotics, so I believe these cost issues will be negligible for the business in light of its grand strategy that is focused on the long term and on optimizing operations incrementally.

The main robots currently already in operation include Hercules for heavy lifting, Proteus for autonomous mobility, Sparrow for vision-guided picking, Robin/Cardinal for sorting/packing, Vulcan for tactile-sensitive handling, and Sequoia for high-density/automated pantry. Recently, Amazon's proprietary DeepFleet AI has boosted robot fleet efficiency by approximately 10%.

Understandably, Amazon has sought to retrain about 700,000 employees, but highly automated warehouses have already seen approximately 10% headcount reductions. It's early to make full predictions on social and regulatory backlash if Amazon seeks to replace human labor more aggressively, but given the current directive to retrain rather than outright replace, some balance will likely be instigated by management by ethical and structural choice rather than regulatory need. Although operating margins could rise more dramatically with a fully automated system, product demand could trough if there are more general macroeconomic difficulties with per capita annual income across Amazon's core operating regions.

Amazon is not a total outlier here. Already, Walmart (WMT) is exhibiting rapid automation and is targeting about a 20% fulfillment cost reduction by 2026, JD.com (JD) already has ultra-automated warehouses, and Ocado (OTCPK:OCDGF) (OTCPK:OCDDY) is already operating a robotics-as-a-service model. In other words, Amazon's robotics initiatives are not here entirely as a means to carve itself a distinct moat, but also just to stay operationally relevant and resilient amid the current technological disruption. Robotics may not have permeated all of society yet, but in a decade or two, those who don't invest now are likely to face significant competitive pressures that can easily stall returns and even put smaller companies out of business—based purely on margin pressures forcing stalled demand.

Amazon Is Still Ripe to Buy

Although many AI stocks have become overextended right now, Amazon is an exception. Although the stock is trading above the 50-week moving average, in this instance that's perfectly fine and investable. The 14-week Relative Strength Index ("RSI") is under 60, indicating that the investment is not overbought. In other words, there's more room in this bull run and Amazon is not impeded by valuation constraints severely. Indeed, we may not be looking at a value investment anymore, but we can still buy world-class resilient growth with expanding normalized earnings per share growth rates over the next few years due to management's shrewd focus on holistic margin expansion. In the medium and long term, robotics is going to be the core driver that sustains this growth and even allows it to moderately increase further. On the other hand, revenue growth rate expansion is slightly more muted, given scale constraints which are perfectly natural for a company of Amazon's size.

In one year, we're most likely looking at $7 in mid-2026 trailing 12-month earnings per share in a base case, with a P/E slightly higher than present due to a macro bull market from low interest rates and potentially established geopolitical stability. Amazon's current normalized P/E is about 35, so I'm predicting a conservative 37.5 for mid-2026. Therefore, my 12-month price target is $262.50. The current price is $219.50, so the implied upside is nearly 20%.

Market & Regulatory Constraints Could Impede Returns

In Fiscal 2024, Amazon's adjusted free cash flow was $36.2 billion, so robotics already consumes ~19% of annual free cash flow (given roughly $7 billion in robotics capex for Fiscal 2024). There are medium-term and long-term risks with cost overruns and slower return on investment that could cause a cash-flow squeeze and inhibit returns. But given management's reputation for excellence in capital allocation and the oversight of Bezos, I don't think this risk is acute, even though it presents the biggest downside from strategic error.

Return on robotics investment is also stalled by Amazon Labor Union and EU counterparts seeking bargaining rights on the pace of AI and robotics adoption. I expect we will see more of this until the market stabilizes or some form of universal basic income reduces the incentives of robotics regulation. Also, the mergers and acquisitions pipeline is becoming constrained. The European Commission performed an in-depth probe into the $1.7 billion iRobot deal which shows how regulators are ready to block robotics acquisitions; this limits Amazon's return horizon through constraining its acquisition of moat-defining automation intellectual property.

Conclusion: Buy

Amazon's stock price is a little high right now, but with a company as exceptional as this, that doesn't really matter. There's a lot more upside to come based on the stock's momentum, macroeconomic factors to support sentiment, and strong medium-term margin expansion prospects that fortify the bull case. Even with constraints from regulation (which are valid until macroeconomic stability is achieved in light of robotics), the upside far outweighs this and the return on investment may just be more gradual, not stopped. I forecast about a 20% price return for the stock in the next 12 months. Amazon's robotics revolution has already begun, and we have far more to look forward to.

data.title: June Jobs Stunner: 147,000 Jobs Created, Unemployment Rate Lowest Since January
data.description:
The June jobs report came in much better than expected. The U.S. economy created 147,000 jobs last month, sharply above the consensus of 106,000, and above May's upwardly revised 144,000 figure.

The unemployment rate fell significantly to 4.117%, down from 4.244% a month ago. The jobless rate is now the lowest since January, breaking a streak of sequential increases.

Average hourly earnings came in below estimates, however, rising 0.2% in June, below the forecast of +0.3%, bringing the year-on-year increase to 3.7%, two-tenths of a percentage point under consensus.

While nonfarm payrolls were ahead of estimates, private job creation was notably soft. Per the BLS, just 74,000 private-sector positions were added. Government payrolls jumped by 73,000, the biggest increase since March of 2024, though all of the gains were due to growth in state and local governments, not at the federal level.

Elsewhere, manufacturing payrolls fell by 7,000 for the second consecutive month, highlighting ongoing weakness in that cyclical sector. The labor force participation rate dropped to 62.3%, its lowest level since November 2022, signaling that fewer Americans are either working or actively seeking work.

Investors should also take note of the household survey, which is used to determine the unemployment rate. It revealed a net gain of 93,000 jobs in June, a notable rebound from the sharp 696,000 decline in the prior month. Also bullish for the macro was the significant shift in employment composition: full-time workers increased by 282,000, while part-time workers fell by 367,000.

The 8:30 a.m. ET data deluge was heavy—initial jobless claims declined for the third straight week, suggesting layoffs remain low despite the softer hiring environment. And that’s about the conclusion we got from both the Challenger Gray Job Cuts announcement and ADP Private Payrolls earlier this week. Also recall that the May JOLTS report signaled a surprising increase in the number of open positions.

All told, there’s now a 5% chance of a Fed rate cut to come later this month, down from 25% from before the report, and there’s even a 1-in-5 chance of the FOMC being on hold through September.

A Strong June Jobs Report

X Poll: Fintwit Was Too Bearish

Labor Market Stability

Large Drop in the Unemployment Rate, Lowest Since January

Monthly Employment Growth is Steady

Job Changes by Industry

Government Job Growth in June

Positive Payroll Revisions

Average Hourly Earnings Continues Dipping

U.S. Recession Chance Plunges to 20%

Despite the jobs report blowout, equity futures didn’t move much. The S&P 500 futures (SPX) indicated a slightly higher open, with strength in small-cap stocks.

Treasury rates soared, though. The yield on the 2-year Treasury note (US2Y) jumped about 10 basis points, with the US Dollar Index (DXY) climbing by 0.5%.

Stocks Strong Heading Into the Holiday Weekend

Yields Jumped After the 8:30 A.M. Data

The Dollar Ripped Post-NFP

Looking ahead, it’s a light data week on tap. We’ll have to wait until the second full week of July for inflation data and June Retail Sales. What’s more, bank earnings begin rolling in on Tuesday, July 15. So, until then, eyes will be on trade deals and other macro concerns.

Still, we will get nuggets from the June NFIB Small Business Optimism survey, along with key clues from last month’s FOMC Minutes. As always, jobless claims are in focus for nearly real-time information on the health of the labor market.

Data On Tap

The Bottom Line

It was a surprisingly strong June payrolls report. The headline number was above forecasts, while revisions to the previous two months were positive. The unemployment rate dropped materially, although private payrolls were lighter than expected. The U.S. economy continues to defy bearish predictions, and the Fed is likely on hold for the next two months.

data.title: The Debate's Heating Up At The ECB, According To Released Minutes
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

The minutes of the ECB’s June meeting confirmed the message that President Christine Lagarde tried to convey during the press conference: downside risks to growth and the downward revisions to the Bank's own inflation forecasts were the main reasons for the European Central Bank to cut interest rates again. Interestingly, there had been two fundamental debates: one on whether monetary policy was now still neutral or already accommodative, despite earlier comments by President Lagarde debunking this concept; and+ one on whether or not inflation undershooting should be a concern. This latter debate would not have happened if the ECB had a price level target instead of an inflation target.

Here are some highlights from the minutes

Inflation undershooting, part 1. “Members discussed the extent to which the projected temporary undershooting of the inflation target was a concern. Concerns were expressed that following the downward revisions to annual inflation for both 2025 and 2026, inflation was projected to be below the target for 18 months, which could be considered as extending into the medium term.”

Inflation undershooting, part 2. “The risk of a sustained undershooting of the inflation target was seen as limited unless there was a sharp deterioration in labour market conditions.”

Neutral or accommodative. “It was contended that, after seven rate cuts, interest rates were now firmly in neutral territory and possibly already in accommodative territory. It was argued that this was also suggested by the upturn in credit growth and by the bank lending survey.”

Not everyone agreed with the rate cut decision, part 1. “Almost all members supported the proposal made by Mr Lane to lower the three key ECB interest rates by 25 basis points.”

Not everyone agreed with the rate cut decision, part 2. “At the same time, a few members saw a case for keeping interest rates at their current levels. The near-term temporary inflation undershoot should be looked through, since it was mostly due to volatile factors such as lower energy prices and a stronger exchange rate, which could easily reverse.”

Markets can still drive the ECB. “In this context, it was recalled that the staff projections were conditioned on a market curve that embedded a 25 basis point rate cut in June and about 50 basis points of cuts in total by the end of 2025.”

We expect the ECB to cut again in September

In its policy assessment published earlier this week, the ECB stressed the importance of scenario analysis. An argument that perfectly fits into the current situation. A situation of high policy and economic uncertainty. With this in mind, it appears that the ECB will now want to take a step back and, at least temporarily, halt the current rate-cutting cycle. As Lagarde said at the June press conference, “the ECB is in a good place” now.

This ‘good place’ has become somewhat less comfortable recently due to the continued strengthening of the euro. Not only does the stronger euro act as an additional tariff on top of the current trade tensions, but it also bears the risk of more inflation undershooting. Consequently, chances are high that the ECB will have to leave the ‘good place’ soon and continue cutting rates.

Driven by the stronger currency, the only question for the ECB currently seems to be when and by how much and not if to continue cutting rates. In fact, in case of a re-escalation of trade tensions and the subsequent weakening of the eurozone growth outlook, further rate cuts would come faster and at a larger magnitude. Should the trade story move on without a strong escalation and further turmoil, and inflation continues its recent disinflationary trend, but fiscal stimulus brings back growth and inflation, in 2026 at the latest, we would see the ECB cutting only one more time in September.

All in all, today’s minutes indicate that the discussion at the ECB about next steps has heated up. The strengthening of the euro could be a welcome argument to continue cutting rates, while at the same time, the question of whether or not to simply allow for inflation undershooting to compensate for previous overshooting will get more attention.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: NIE: Blended CEF That Delivers Both Growth And Income
data.description:
Overview

Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NIE) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its blended portfolio of equities and convertible securities. This blended approach allows NIE to participate in both capital appreciation alongside the rest of the market, as well as a superior income potential. I think a fund like NIE can be efficiently used for investors that want to put an emphasis on income and build a position that can serve as a hedge to soften a portfolio's vulnerability to the price swing of traditional equities. When a fund provides a superior dividend and consistent track record of payouts, it can psychologically make it a lot easier to remain invested and mentally tune out the market noise.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NIE's price has only increased by about 2.5%. Since the fund still maintains exposure to equities, NIE did decline throughout March and April alongside the rest of the market. However, the fund was able to successfully recover from the decline, which reinforces its ability to participate in the growth of the market. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 11.7%. NIE offers a generous starting dividend yield of about 8.4%, which makes it a powerful tool for income generation.

NIE is capable of paying out a large yield because of the fund's inclusion of an option strategy. While option strategies tend to cap upside price appreciation, NIE has proven that it differentiates itself by providing shareholders with the best of both worlds, income and capital appreciation. Since the fund's portfolio has an income component with its convertible securities, NIE has demonstrated a bit of a sensitivity to interest rate fluctuations. The fund has an inception dating back to 2007 and has an expense ratio of 1.12%. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that NIE implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest overview, NIE has total net assets of $725.6M that is spread across a diverse range of assets. NIE takes a blended approach by including exposure to both common equities and convertible securities. Convertible securities are essentially bonds or preferred stock that can be converted to common shares, as the name implies. These securities are often used to generate a higher level of income than what traditional equities can produce.

If one of these securities has the potential to experience a large amount of capital appreciation or valuation growth, the fund may choose to convert it into common equity as a way to allow NIE to capture the highest level of growth possible. The breakdown of NIE's portfolio leans more heavily on common equities but convertible securities still account for a sizeable portion of its strategy.

  • Common Equities: 57.46%
  • Convertible Securities: 35.70%
  • Cash: 6.87%

When looking at the breakdown of NIE by sector, we can see that Information Technology companies make up the largest bulk of the fund at 25.82%. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Health Care investments, accounting for 13.78% and 12.33% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up nearly 52% of NIE's total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on NIE's daily price change and total return potential.

The fund provides instant diversification across many well known large-cap companies. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 3.56% of assets. This is followed by varying weights to other companies like Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META) just to name a few. While many of NIE's top holdings may have experienced double-digit growth over the last twelve months, NIE has not. This can likely be attributed to the fund's inclusion of an option strategy.

While I could not locate any fund documents that clarify the exact option strategy, the fund writes options as a way to boost the income received from a holding. NIE tends to write call options valued at 70% of the value of each position, which still allows for some price appreciation to be experienced. The unfortunate byproduct of options is that NIE's growth is essentially limited to whatever the selected strike price is, which is why NIE is likely to consistently underperform its own holdings.

Just to demonstrate the fund's efficiency, we can see that it has a long track record of success over the last ten years. NIE's price has increased by 23.5% but when including distributions, the total return jumps up above 217% over the last decade. This means that the fund has been able to deliver attractive price growth alongside the high distributions.

Valuation

Since NIE operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, NIE currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 8.4%. Looking back over the last ten year history below, we can see that NIE has consistently traded in the discount territory and has never touched the premium to NAV territory. For reference, NIE has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 10% over the last three years. However, I believe that NIE still trades at an attractive valuation when we consider its consistent performance.

In order to get better clarity into the fund's performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.21 per share, while net realized gains landed at $4.49 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income sits at the highest level seen over the last five years. It's possible that the higher net investment income levels can be attributed to the higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher amount of interest income that the fund can collect from debt securities.

Since net investment income levels are typically low, NIE seems heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel its NAV growth and support the distributions. The only problem is that the rate of net realized gains are directly associated with the overall direction of market indexes. For instance, NIE experienced a large net realized loss of $4.02 per share for 2023, which is in-line with the market's decline that was experienced throughout 2022. This dependence on positive market conditions is one of the reasons why the NAV still remains below its pre-pandemic level. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $30.91 per share
  • 2022: $30.32 per share
  • 2023: $22.43 per share
  • 2024: $23.82 per share
  • 2025: $26.52 per share

However, I do commend the fund's ability to regain some positive NAV momentum from its lows of 2023. NIE has been able to capture large net realized gains from its equities whenever market indexes are trended upward. If interest rates are cut within the next few quarters, this can serve as a positive catalyst for markets and help take NIE's price higher. Therefore, I have confidence that the fund will be able to experience NAV growth when held for the long-term.

Risk Profile

Since NIE has exposure to some convertible securities that produce income, the fund has demonstrated some sensitivity to shifts in the interest rate environment. For instance, we can see how NIE shares an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate below. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic, NIE's price rapidly appreciated as a result. An environment of lower interest rates meant that higher yielding assets like convertible securities became much more valuable for investors. Additionally, companies experienced healthier operating margins as a result of the lower cost of debt.

Conversely, NIE's price retracted from the highs as interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. Therefore, there's always a risk that we remain in an elevated interest rate environment, and it keeps the underlying equities and convertibles suppressed. The Fed has kept interest rates unchanged for their fourth straight meeting as they continue to monitor macroeconomic data. I think the Fed is trying to avoid cutting interest rates too early since markets still haven't felt the full impact of President Trump's tariff proposals. The Fed still wants to assess the changes to consumer spending levels, unemployment, and inflationary data.

Additionally, there's always the risk that technology retracts from its current highs. Since NIE is most heavily concentrated to the technology sector, the fund's pricing may be negatively impacted if sentiment around the sector changes. Investors are optimistic around the future growth of the AI sector but that can shift at any moment. For instance, we already saw the negative impact that Chinese competitor, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), had on the technology sector when they released an LLM (large language model) that was just as efficient as ChatGPT at a fraction of the cost.

Dividend

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.50 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.4%. If we multiply this quarterly payout by four, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $2.00 per share, assuming that payouts remain the same. Referring back to the same annual report, NIE has demonstrated that it is entirely capable of producing earnings in excess of the payout amount, so I am fairly optimistic that NIE will not cut the distribution any time soon. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2021: $7.54 per share
  • 2022: $2.68 per share
  • 2023: ($3.91) per share
  • 2024: $3.39 per share
  • 2025: $4.70 per share

As we can see, NIE has been able to outpace the current distribution amount in four of the last five years. This sort of stability makes NIE a solid choice for investors that want a secure source of supplemental dividend income. It also helps that the fund doesn't try to implement an overly-generous distribution policy that can be harmful to the growth of the NAV.

One of the benefits to NIE is that the distributions tend to be mostly tax-efficient. However, 'tax-efficient' doesn't necessarily mean that all distributions will be tax-free. The latest section19(a) notice indicates that 79.2% of the distributions YTD have been paid out with net realized long-term capital gains, which are classified as qualified dividends. However, there is still a portion of the distributions that are paid out using net investment income or net realized short-term capital gains, which are both classified as ordinary dividends and have the heaviest tax burden. I would consult with a tax professional to determine how a breakdown such as this would impact you before initiating a position.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NIE is a solid fund that offers investors an attractive value proposition. NIE still trades at a decent discount to NAV and the fund has a good track record of being able to provide both capital appreciation and consistent income over the last decade. Reporting indicates that NIE can also generate an efficient level of earnings that have no problem outpacing the distributions that are paid. Therefore, the fund is able to maintain consistent payouts for investors, making it a great choice for reliable income generation.

data.title: Can Landlords Survive Mamdani's Rent Freeze?
data.description:
Rent control is often mischaracterized as landlords versus tenants in a sort of Marxian proletariat versus bourgeoisie.

On the surface, this characterization makes sense, as landlords would generally want higher rent while tenants would generally want lower rent. It gets far more complex as we pull back the layers of the onion.

Rent control has been thrust into the spotlight as Zohran Mamdani, a democratic socialist, bested former governor Andrew Cuomo in the NYC Mayoral primary. A cornerstone of Mamdani’s campaign has been a pledge to freeze apartment rents in the city, with rents bordering on unaffordable.

This potentially has massive implications for apartment REITs so we shall dig in. As always, we will remain non-political in our discussion.

Key topics analyzed in this article:

  • Mamdani rent freeze policy
  • Potential for rent freezes to become widespread
  • Rent control mechanics
  • REITs with significant exposure to potentially rent controlled apartments
  • Impact on apartment REITs

Let us begin with the specific policy proposed by Mamdani and then move on to rent control more broadly. Per Mamdani’s platform webpage, he intends to freeze rent on rent-stabilized apartments.

Specifically, there is a board that governs rental rates for rent-stabilized apartments. In recent years, this board has approved rent increases each year that roughly match inflation. Mamdani intends to elect board members that favor rent freezes, such that year over year rent change would be 0%.

This policy would have little direct impact on apartment REITs because publicly traded REITs own almost exclusively market rate apartments as opposed to affordable housing or rent-stabilized apartments.

AvalonBay (AVB), for example, has substantial exposure to NYC with its properties mapped below.

However, AVB’s average monthly rent is well over $3,000 while rent-controlled apartments in NYC are closer to $1,500. Thus, a freeze on rent increases on rent-controlled units would have minimal impact on AVB because it is such a different segment.

However, there is potential for rent control to become significantly more widespread, both geographically and by market segment.

Potential For Rent Freezes Or Similar Policies To Become More Widespread

Mamdani has tapped into a broad frustration about the high cost of living. His rent freeze policy is arguably what launched him into popularity and despite the Mayoral election still being 4 months away, he is already at 70.4% on Polymarket betting odds.

Polymarket odds are to be taken with a grain of salt as the bidders which inform the odds tend to skew into certain demographics (likely younger) and those demographics may be more favorable to Mamdani.

So the real chances of a Mamdani victory might not actually be 70%, but they are certainly high and likely above 50%.

Cost of living frustration is not unique to NYC, and seeing Mamdani’s popularity could embolden politicians throughout the country to foster similar stances. Thus, while Mamdani’s currently proposed policy has minimal impact on apartment REITs, it behooves us to consider the potential for broader rent control.

Rent control can extend beyond apartments classified as affordable housing. For example, belonghome notes that:

The Tenant Protection Act caps rent increases for most residential tenants in California. Landlords can’t raise rent more than 10% total or 5% + CPI increase (whichever is lower) over a 12-month period.

10% is a big year-over-year change that is unlikely to come into play in the next few years, although it is worth noting that 20% rental rate growth was common circa 2021-2022.

With enough political willpower, that 10% could be amended to 2% or even 0%. That would have major implications, so we want to have a deeper understanding about what that means for apartment owners as well as tenants.

Rent Control Mechanics

Rent control directly affects the price landlords are allowed to charge, and in this first order impact it does keep rent lower. However, there are 2 ripple effects which make the overall impact much less clear.

  • Disincentivized maintenance capex
  • Disincentivized new development

Landlords are constantly in competition with other landlords of similar price points. They are incentivized to maintain their buildings because tenants are more likely to go with the nicer/cleaner/safer place to live.

In other words, capex can actually increase NOI by driving revenue more than the incremental cost. Many REITs, such as NexPoint Residential (NXRT) base their business model around this concept. They buy poorly maintained apartments, and make some smart renovations that increase rent a couple hundred dollars a month for an overall IRR in the 15%-28% range.

With rent control in place, this sort of capex is discouraged. No landlord is going to want to put in thousands of dollars of capex into each unit if they cannot raise rent for doing so.

Similarly, development cap rates in apartments are generally quite low. In 2022, developers underwrote to 4% cap rates. Today, development cap rates are closer to 5.0%-6.5%, but that is still a fairly low return for the risk of development. However, development is made worth it by new properties often experiencing significant rent growth over time.

Thus, the going in cap rate of say 6% can become an 8% or 10% return on invested capital over time. If you take away the rent growth or cap it at 2% through rent control, it disincentivizes development.

Overall Impact Of Rent Control Policy

With the ripple impacts in mind, it is much less clear what the actual outcome of rent control is.

Some believe it actually benefits landlords as they can keep cash flowing while significantly reducing capex spend. New York City and major cities in California are the areas which have most embraced rent control historically, and each of these areas feature extremely high rent and housing shortage.

Essex Property Trust (ESS) is an apartment REIT that has been concentrated in California for decades and has had to battle rent control in various forms.

It has also been quite successful, racking up a strong long-term shareholder return.

Rent control, like most economic policies, involves a complex web of interactions. It is very difficult to disaggregate the various forces that lead to events. We cannot determine if the housing shortage in historically rent controlled areas is related to rent control or if it is simply a matter of the high population density of these areas which results in land scarcity.

As such, I have no opinion on the merits of rent control in terms of whether it is good or bad as a policy. We can merely consider the potential impacts on apartment REITs listed below:

  • Lower rent growth in the near term
  • Lower capex
  • Lower competing development
  • Mixed impact on NOI

On the flip side, we can also look at areas that have gone the opposite direction. Texas has intentionally low regulations, and ample land, which has encouraged development. Rents are allowed to go sky-high but are also tamped down by competing supply.

Per the June data from ApartmentList, rental rates are down 6.4% in Austin and down 3.2% in San Antonio.

This happened despite both cities having healthy population and job growth. Texas exposed apartment REITs BSR REIT (OTCPK:BSRTF) and Camden (CPT) are currently feeling the negative impacts of high supply.

On the positive side, these Texas markets have also experienced incredible boom times when construction activity is naturally in a low period.

Wrapping It Up

The financial media is often overly focused on the first order impacts of policy, but economics always involves feedback mechanisms and ripple effects. In giving proper weight to the full web of mechanisms, it is entirely unclear whether apartment REITs perform better in the laissez-faire of Texas, or the more controlling policies such as those proposed by Mamdani.

So rather than trying to buy or avoid apartment REITs exposed to certain political ideologies, I think forward performance will be much more determined by individual company operations, fundamentals and valuation.

We are still somewhat light on apartment REIT exposure presently as we await further data on how well the wave of deliveries gets absorbed. The sector is well positioned for strong NOI growth going forward, it is just hard to tell if that will start in late 2025, early 2026, or late 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Synopsys Cleared For China Sales, But Not For This Valuation
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:SNPS) is poised to rebound more quickly than the Street expects, thanks to a reopened China channel, an $8 billion backlog, and growing demand for its AI-driven design tools, yet its share price already reflects a premium multiple that assumes flawless execution and downplays fresh debt from the pending Ansys deal.

Synopsys’ Ban Reversal and the Forecast Gap

Synopsys’ last two quarters tell a pretty straightforward story: the company absorbed a sudden, government-imposed hit to its China sales, saw that hit reversed six weeks later by the same regulator, and now trades as if the reversal never happened. In late May the Commerce Department halted virtually all electronic-design-automation exports to China; in Q2 the firm’s China revenue fell 29% year on year, cutting 1/6 of a business that had produced 16% of fiscal-2024 sales. Management pulled detailed guidance, analysts assumed the freeze would last all year, and a $160 million shortfall was penciled into consensus models.

Yet on July 3, Commerce rescinded the ban, restoring the legal pathway for Synopsys, Cadence (CDNS) and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY) to resume shipments. Although management's full-year targets of $6.75 to $6.8 billion in revenue and $15.11 to $15.19 in non-GAAP earnings per share have yet to be adjusted, the sell-side narrative still assumes mid-teens year-on-year China declines in the back half of fiscal 2025. The valuation argument really comes down to the gap between what regulators now permit and what the forecasts still project.

Because the ban had been active for only 1 of the 3 months in the April-to-July quarter, Synopsys managed to post overall revenue of $1.604 billion, 10% higher than a year earlier, and, more tellingly, backlog climbed $400 million to $8.1 billion, signaling that demand moved into deferred status rather than disappearing. Historically, around 2/3 of deferred orders convert within 2 quarters once a restriction lifts; if that pattern holds, between $100 and $150 million in China bookings should appear on the income statement before the October fiscal year-end, adding roughly 2% to full-year revenue and $0.20 to $0.25 to earnings per share at a software-weighted incremental margin of 20% to 25%. None of that pickup appears in current consensus estimates. In effect, models treat the ban as still in force even though purchase orders can legally flow again.

One reason the market hesitates is the depth of Chinese reliance on Synopsys verification tools. During the 5-week blackout first-pass tape-out success reportedly dropped from 24% to 14% because domestic substitutes lag several technology nodes behind. Beijing’s $48 billion Big Fund III explicitly targets home-grown EDA, but Empyrean, the leading local candidate, was added to the U.S. Entity List in December, restricting its access to new IP and foreign talent. China therefore has strong practical reasons to keep Synopsys software flowing until credible replacements exist, and Washington, having discovered that cutting off EDA threatened its own leverage in wider trade talks, now seems content to let sales resume. So long as this uneasy truce holds, Synopsys can work through its deferred backlog, yet analysts still model a sustained revenue hole.

Management itself is cautious: it refused to lift formal guidance after the rollback, citing execution risk and the time Chinese procurement teams need to restart contracting cycles. Even so, the call highlighted strengths that the China narrative obscures. Design-IP revenue rose 21% year on year; AI accelerators such as DSO.ai and VSO.ai shortened placement-and-route runs from days to hours, boosting customer productivity; and hardware-assisted verification booked record demand as system-on-chip complexity escalated. Stripping out a $0.28 real-estate gain and a $0.06 net-interest cost linked to the $10 billion bond issue that will finance the Ansys acquisition, quarterly earnings landed at $3.00 a share, roughly $0.05 above consensus.

Synopsys: High Multiples, Higher Leverage, Limited Safety Net

In terms of valuation, FAST Graphs pegs intrinsic value around $293, only 56% of the current quote. In that view, even a full China rebound would not rescue holders if multiples revert to long-term means. The bears would also point to the $10 billion in debt: interest expense shaved $0.06 off last quarter’s earnings and lowered projected fiscal-2025 free cash flow to about $1.3 billion, so merger synergies must arrive on schedule to justify the leverage.

The merger in question, Synopsys’ $35 billion takeover of Ansys, has cleared every regulator except China’s State Administration for Market Regulation. Talks are described as advanced, and Beijing rarely blocks U.S.-U.S. deals unless a direct national-security angle emerges, yet the timing remains uncertain. If SAMR signs off before year-end, Synopsys will add a multiphysics simulation stack that pushes its reach from chip layout into complete system verification. Management estimates that the union expands addressable market size by $10 to $12 billion; even without heavy synergy assumptions, the combined offering could justify a higher contract value with hyperscalers and automotive OEMs. Still, until the deal closes, the debt is real and the incremental revenue is theoretical, so the clock ticks on integration risk.

Recurring revenue presents another subtle risk. Subscriptions have been hovering around the 80% level of sales, but in Q2 the mix dipped because upfront Design-IP licenses surged. CFO Shelagh Glaser called the swing ordinary timing; nonetheless, if one-time streams continue to swell, free-cash-flow visibility could fade. Part of the bullish angle is that subscription dollars cushion Synopsys against the boom-bust chip cycle, a cushion that thins out once hardware sales and royalty checks start driving the growth.

Still, the backdrop isn’t falling apart. AI servers and next-gen car chips keep cap-ex rising at a high-single-digit clip, and because Synopsys charges at design kickoff rather than when wafers ship, it pockets its cut early, enough to post 10% revenue growth even with the export door half-shut. If policy risk stays neutral, deferred backlog should convert; if policy tilts more favorable, I think Chinese foundries will rush to recover lost tape-out cycles, potentially accelerating revenue recognition.

Even after a 1-year return down around 14%, the stock still trades at a premium.

The blended P/E sits at 36.08 against a “fair value” multiple of 15, implying fair value of about $217 and a future price of roughly that aforementioned $293 if the market eventually drifts back toward historical norms. On FAST Graphs that gap translates into an estimated annual return of –22 percent (see previous FG chart above). The trailing figure is even richer, 39.63 for fiscal 2024, while consensus puts the forward P/E at 34.61 for 2025, 30.97 for 2026 and 26.53 for 2027.

Those forward numbers already assume EPS growth running in the low- to mid-teens (14.52 percent in fiscal 2025, 11.75 percent in 2026 and a rebound to 16.73 percent in 2027). If that earnings curve wobbles or the multiple merely migrates toward the sector’s mid-twenties norm, today’s $523 share price has plenty of room to compress before value buyers get interested.

Here’s the quick math I walked through. First, I estimated the extra EPS Synopsys might get if Chinese orders start flowing again. Then I translated that bump into a share-price range at today’s 36× multiple and, finally, asked what happens if the market decides the stock should trade closer to a mid-20s multiple instead.

As of this analysis, the pre-market trades are printing around $554.89. Add the mid-case $0.20 in extra EPS and were already getting close to drifting into the high $560s; push the full $0.25 and you land near $569. Using the Street’s forward multiple of roughly 34.6× (see above), the base case lines up pretty close with the previous close price around $526, and even the full China kicker only stretches that to about $547, not bad, but nothing that transforms the story if sentiment cools.

Once you model compression to 30x or 26.5x, still richer than FAST Graphs’ “fair” band, the safety net shrinks. Without a China boost, implied value drops to roughly $456 and $403; even the full $0.25 in extra earnings only drags those figures back to around $460 and $410. Let the multiple sink into the mid-twenties and the stock ends up in the high-$370s without the China boost, and only scrapes into the low-$390s even with it.

Bottom line: the China restart can add $0.20 to $0.25 to next year’s earnings, but valuation math does most of the heavy lifting. The stock holds up if investors stay comfortable paying north of 30× forward earnings. If they revisit the mid-20s, the price falls, China bump or not. That makes the next 2 quarters of China reorder data and any clue from SAMR on the Ansys deal critical, because they’re the only near-term catalysts big enough to shift either the earnings line or the market’s chosen multiple before Synopsys closes its fiscal year in October.

Thus, the company now straddles two timelines: the export-control shock that drove share-price volatility is already history; the revenue it deferred remains future. The debt raised for Ansys burdens the balance sheet today; the cash flows meant to service it depend on a regulatory green light tomorrow. Wall Street’s models appear to discount the positive future while capitalizing the negative present, producing a skewed risk profile. Given the available evidence of rising backlog, intact guidance, legal clearance for shipments, and Chinese designers acknowledging productivity losses without Synopsys tools, the odds of at least a partial snap-back look higher than the odds of an equally severe new ban. Yet that is a probability judgment, not a guarantee, and it leaves room for both upside surprise and valuation compression.

Final Takeaway Summary

Rating: Hold. I see real upside from the China restart and backlog conversion, but Synopsys, Inc. stock already prices in a lofty 30-plus P/E; if that multiple drifts back toward the mid-twenties, the valuation math overwhelms the $0.20–0.25 EPS bump. Until we get clear proof of Chinese reorder momentum or SAMR approves the Ansys deal, I’d stay on the sidelines and watch how those two catalysts play out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Philip Morris: Time To Take Profits
data.description:
Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM) has shown extraordinary resilience during the April sell-off and in the months that follow, causing shares to surge to new highs. In my opinion, Philip Morris’ earnings potential is now more than fully reflected in its earnings-based valuation factor and I have decided to take profits and recycle my investment proceeds into British American Tobacco (BTI) for a higher yield and potentially better capital return potential. In my opinion, Philip Morris is going to continue to generate strong EPS growth going forward, but shares are extremely richly priced, especially in direct comparison to other tobacco stocks. Due to valuation considerations – PM is now trading in excess of 21X FY 2026 earnings – I believe locking in profits is the smart thing to do.

Previous Rating

Philip Morris was a hold position for me in my last work in April 2025 – No Margin Of Safety – because of the company’s tilt towards fast-growing emerging markets. The strategic focus on rapidly expanding emerging markets generated the precondition for above-average EPS growth for the tobacco company, and Philip Morris was returning a decent amount of cash back to shareholders. However, PM is now priced well above my fair value estimate, making this a perfect time to sell into the strength and lock in a handsome profit.

Strong Alternative Products Growth Now Fully Baked Into PM's Valuation

Philip Morris’ business is growing, despite secular challenges to the company’s core combustible business. However, since Philip Morris has strong exposure to emerging markets where combustible adoption is still strong, and the tobacco company is seeing rapid expansion in its alternative products category – especially IQOS, ZYN and VEEL (the firm's e-vapor brand) – I believe that Philip Morris' valuation factor leaves little room for more upside.

Alternative products, like ZYN-branded nicotine pouches, IQOS heated tobacco items and VEEP vape products, are key growth drivers at Philip Morris and the company has seen a massive boost to its volume growth in the last year. In the first-quarter, the tobacco company benefited from a boost to its shipments volume of 3.9% Y/Y as well as more than 10% organic revenue growth, mainly due to new smoke-free products. In Q1'25, about 42% of all revenue in Philip Morris' portfolio came from its smoke-free, alternative products, showing 2 PP growth compared to the previous quarter.

The company's alternative product categories are therefore also leading the company's portfolio in terms of growth. In fact, new products in the smoke-free category are driving Philip Morris' entire top line, and the tobacco company has seen some considerable upside momentum in terms of gross profits as well. In the most recent quarter, Philip Morris generated solid 20.4% Y/Y revenue growth in this specific category in Q1'25 with its gross profits growing in excess of 33%.

Despite fundamental challenges to Philip Morris' core business model – selling traditional tobacco products – the company has been able, with the help of new products like IQOS, ZYN or VEEV, to accelerate its shipment volume growth. As a result, the tobacco firm is seeing overall favorable revenue and income trends in its business. In the last three years, Philip Morris' net income surged 16.6% while its revenue grew by about 25%. While Philip Morris is growing at a healthy clip, I believe the company is now overvalued based off of its expected earnings.

Dividend Growth Rates: BTI Vs. Tobacco Rivals

Altria (MO) still has the best dividend growth in the industry group and out-performed both Philip Morris as well as British American Tobacco: Altria generated close to 19% dividend growth in the last five years compared to growth rate of 12.5% for Philip Morris and 8.6% for British American Tobacco. From a dividend growth perspective, the tobacco stock to own would be Altria... which I discussed at length here: A Solid Dividend Play.

Recycling Proceeds From PM Into BTI

Philip Morris is now trading at a 21.3X forward earnings multiplier, which is more than twice as much as what the earnings potential of British American Tobacco is valued at. I therefore decided to sell my shares in Philip Morris this week and recycle my proceeds into BTI, which is not only trading at a much lower earnings multiplier of 9.9X FY 2026 earnings, but also has a much higher dividend yield.

Philip Morris' yield is down to 3.2%, due to massive expansion in the company's P/E ratio, compared to 6.3% for British American Tobacco. I like BTI for both its momentum with its own oral nicotine products, named Velo, and its capital return potential, which I discussed here: A Cheap And Juicy Yield. Since BTI is trading at less than half the P/E ratio than PM, while also having considerable momentum in its alternative products category, I believe investors could potentially earn higher capital returns from British American Tobacco going forward.

In my last work on Philip Morris, I calculated a fair value of $107, based off of a historical P/E ratio of ~15X for the tobacco company. Today, shares of Philip Morris trade in excess of $175, so are, according to my calculation, overvalued by approximately 64%. At the current time, I believe Philip Morris' alternative products momentum is more than fully realized in the firm's valuation and I therefore see an unfavorable risk profile, especially for longer term investors.

Risks With Philip Morris

The biggest risk for Philip Morris, as I see it, relates to slowing momentum in emerging markets as well as in weakening adoption of new smoke-free products like ZYN or IQOS that currently driving new category revenue and gross profit momentum. What would change my opinion on the tobacco company is if the firm were to trade at a much more reasonable valuation factor (~15X forward earnings) or saw an acceleration in its earnings growth.

Final Thoughts

Philip Morris is growing its revenue, its net income and dividend despite secular challenges to the tobacco industry. The emerging market focus as well as the development of smoke-free products has prevented Philip Morris from a decline in its top line and earnings metrics. However, shares are now so expensive that even Philip Morris’ above-average earnings growth is more than fully baked in the tobacco company’s valuation. In my opinion, it could make sense to recycle proceeds into a lower-P/E tobacco firm, even under the assumption of slower annual EPS growth, given BTI's much higher yield and equally strong momentum in new product sales. British American Tobacco's low P/E ratio also reflects a much higher safety margin compared to Philip Morris in case the company were to see a deterioration of fundamentals (vape bans, slowing new product adoption, weaker volume shipments) in the alternative products business.

data.title: Hard To Imagine Retirement Income Without These 2 Cash Machines
data.description:
As for an income investor, who wants to become financially independent by having a portfolio that funds living expenses just through the generated current income, the journey to get there is quite challenging.

For example, the condition to not rely on portfolio holding sales adds a notable headwind in terms of building up a sufficient portfolio size from which to capture the necessary level of income. Furthermore, without any doubt, the living expenses tend to inflate, which means that the portfolio income has to not only fulfill the pre-defined target, but also deliver some growth in order to preserve the purchasing power. On top of that, if there is a "no asset sale" policy in place, then experiencing an income reduction is fully unacceptable, which, in turn, requires being smart and prudent about investment choices.

However, it is of course not just limitation and negatives. There are multiple benefits from this kind of portfolio objective, which, in my view, make it worth doing the heavy work:

  • Reduced longevity risk (i.e., not outliving the asset base).
  • Opportunity to pass a stable source of income to the next generation (and not just leftovers from non-divested portfolio).
  • Less stressful period of financial independence, where market volatility and asset price risks are taken out of the equation.

To achieve this, the general idea is to invest in securities with as high yields as possible while avoiding income reduction and value decay risks.

It is not easy to balance this as the tendency is of course to consider quite high dividend yielders, which come with higher risks accordingly.

So, the sweet spot area is high-quality value businesses/securities, which offer enticing dividends because of "democratic" valuation multiples. These multiples are lower because there is no expectation by the market that they will deliver strong (Mag 7 like) returns. In the context of the above-mentioned objective, this should not be an issue.

I will share now two high-yielding picks, which, in my view, should be placed at the core of portfolios that seek to deliver on such an objective.

#1 - UTF, 6.9% yield

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:UTF) is a closed end fund, which yields almost 7% and distributes the income on a monthly basis.

UTF has an excellent track-record in terms of delivering stable current income since the moment when it was brought to the public market in 2004. This period includes such market shocks as in the GFC and COVID-19. And what is very important here is that all of these consistent dividends have been distributed without utilizing ROC (return of capital) factor in a way that would impair the underlying value.

It is also true that UTF has not increased the payments since 2017, and, in my view, expecting this to happen (or investing with such an assumption) would not be a smart idea. This is because the Fund really strives to keep both dividends and NAV-preservation in check and with a sufficient margin of safety so that when the markets go down like, say, in the COVID-19, the investors would still be able to collect the planned cash flows.

This goes perfectly in line with the strategy or objectives that I outlined above - i.e., high and durable dividends without value decay.

Now, what has, however, happened in the past year is that UTF has experienced a strong price recovery, which has led to a dividend yield normalization - i.e., convergence to the historical average of around 7%.

In my view, this does not mean that we are buying UTF at an unfavorable moment. Let me quickly provide you with several reasons why this is the case.

First, the lion's share of UTF's portfolio is linked to utility, midstream, telecommunication and other non-cyclical infrastructure assets, which currently play an increasingly important role for portfolio protection. The overall stock market trades at peak valuations, while the level of uncertainty in the system is, arguably, the highest that we have had since the COVID-19 or when the Fed suddenly decided to venture into an aggressive interest rate hiking cycle. In this kind of environment, it is only logical that we pay an extra premium for safety.

Second, it is not only the safety aspects that stem from the non-cyclical and durable income exposure to infrastructure that has motivated investors to flock into UTF. The beauty of most infrastructure assets is that they carry inflation-linked cash flows, which clearly allow de-risking the unpredictable effects from the tariff-driven policy. Plus, it adds another layer of safety for UTF in terms of covering the dividend from net investment income generation.

Third, UTF has taken the extra mile for protecting the underlying asset base and enhancing the sustainability of its monthly distributions. What I mean by that is that there is a significant bias towards investment grade companies. For example, almost the entire Top 10 holdings list is saturated with investment grade balance sheets (and the Top 10 explains ~34% of the total exposure).

UTF is one of those cases, where I feel extremely confident about the future cash flows, which is critical for achieving a "low stress" financial independence.

#2 - PFFA, 9.7%

Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (NYSEARCA:PFFA) plays a different ball game for producing monthly cash flows. It yields 9.7%, which is more than UTF and is fully logical given the higher risk profile.

However, let me say that a higher risk profile does not mean that the risks have exceeded a level that would no longer qualify for durable income and value stability.

The risks come from the fact that PFFA's portfolio largely consists of non investment grade securities, from which around 50% are placed in financial and real estate segments. The combination of non investment grade and cyclical factors does indeed sound aggressive.

But here is the thing.

As opposed to UTF and many other securities, which offer this kind of yield, PFFA has an exclusive focus on preferred share. The essence of preferred shares is that they have inherently lowers risks than equities because they rank higher in the capital structure.

Namely, for a company to stop paying cash to its preferred shareholders, it has to first fully get rid of the common dividend payment.

Then it has to think twice whether it really makes sense to decrease preferred share payments over issuing additional equity because if they go with the former scenario, it would imply an automatic increase in the cost of capital and make new lenders (refinancers) less willing to have any exposure.

Finally, since preferred shares come with fixed payments with pre-set payment dates that have to be serviced, companies cannot just decide what level to pay or not (unless they run in a significant distress and there are no other pragmatic options). This improves the cash flow stability for PFFA.

Moreover, we have to appreciate that PFFA is an actively managed ETF with a modest leverage of 20% to 30% depending on the market circumstances. In my view, to navigate around the sector concentration risk, it is extremely important to cherry-pick the right exposures with a limited downside (income reduction) risk. In other words, it is generally a solid strategy to mitigate sector bias risks with an active security selection process. Also, the 20% to 30% leverage component could enhance the cash flows as the high-probability interest rate cuts take place in the foreseeable future (via cheaper leverage service costs).

Finally, if we look at the dividend payment history all the way since the IPO in 2018, I would say that PFFA has delivered on its objective to distribute enticing monthly cash flows, while preserving the underlying NAV. The Fund has paid out a stable dividend 6 years in a row, but suffered a slight dividend drop right in the peak of COVID-19, which, in my view, is acceptable given the ensuing dividend increases, positive total returns and, importantly, almost 10% yield.

The bottom line

Maximizing yield per unit of risk is a challenging exercise, especially if income reduction and invested capital decay are defined as no-go scenarios.

In the article, I have outlined two instruments, which offer current income streams that should move the needle for most income investors. As discussed, the probability of income cut and/or permanent capital decay risk materialization is extremely low given how robust the underlying fundamentals are.

In my humble opinion, both of these securities could be easily included in the core of retirement income focused portfolios.

data.title: Universal Robina: Refocusing On The ASEAN Region Can Deliver Growth, But Price Is No Longer Attractive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Universal Robina Corporation (OTC:UVRBY)(OTC:UVRBF) is one of the leading consumer food and beverage companies in the Philippines. URC is organized into two operating segments: Branded Consumer Foods [BCF] and Agro-Industrial and Commodity [AIC]. BCF's portfolio comprises a diverse range of products, including snack foods, biscuits, ready-to-drink teas and coffees, noodles, and many others. AIC, meanwhile, sells commodity products such as flour, sugar, and meat.

After URC's disappointing Oceania venture, the company has refocused on the ASEAN market, which, I believe, is a sound strategic shift due to the leadership position they possess in this region. However, despite this bright prospect, I believe that URC's current valuation is unattractive when compared to alternative assets such as the PSEi index. Therefore, I will call a 'Hold' rating for this stock.

FY 2024 Result Discussion

In 2024, URC reported a revenue of ₱161.9B, an increase of 3% compared to last year. EBIT and net income would stand at ₱16.7B and ₱12.2B, down by 4% and 3%, respectively.

The YoY decline in EBIT and net income is due to the URC AIC segment, which saw its EBIT declining by -34% YoY, from a record high ₱8.5B to ₱5.6B. This decline can be attributed to the falling price of sugar, which significantly affected the AIC Sugar and Renewables (SURE), its sugar manufacturing arm.

This decline is a typical correction phase of a commodity cycle. Due to the sugar crisis experienced by the Philippines from 2022 to 2023, sugar manufacturers' production has finally caught up with the demand, thereby correcting the up cycle back to the mean. I expect normalized performance from AIC this year, as supply is being monitored closely by the Philippine Sugar Regulatory Administration (SRA) to protect the interests of farmers and other stakeholders. Excluding the sugar segment, URC's consolidated operating income would've grown by 20%, indicating strong performance from its branded segment. Turning over to the performance of its branded segment, while BCF's revenue grew by only 1.58% YoY, this was offset by the significant growth in the group's consolidated EBIT, which grew by 18.8% YoY.

What impressed me is the result of the BCF international segment, whose EBIT grew by an incredible 43% YoY, indicating that the management's strategy to refocus on ASEAN is gaining traction. I will discuss this in detail later.

Another notable 2024 event for the company was the company's exit from China. I believe this is a good move as the company has never had market leadership in this country. Divesting from China will allow the company to refocus the management's attention and company resources on geographic markets, where they have a market leadership position, specifically the Philippines, Vietnam, Thailand, and Malaysia.

For the year 2025, URC is targeting mid to high-single revenue growth and a net profit growth of a few points behind.

From A Failed Oceania Venture To A More Focused Strategy In ASEAN

In 2014, URC acquired Griffin Food Ltd., a leading snack food company in New Zealand, for approximately ₱26.5 billion or NZ$700M. This transaction was the largest acquisition by URC up to that date. 2 years later, URC would again do another acquisition by acquiring Snackbrand, a market leader in salty snacks in Australia, for a sum of around ₱21.34B or AUD$600M.

From the outset, these acquisitions should be value accretive to URC, as they didn't pay an absurd price. The EV/EBITDA multiple that was used on the Griffin acquisition is around 9x, while around 10x for Snackbrand, which is significantly lower than URC's 24x EV/EBITDA multiple during those times. Additionally, URC acquired a market leader in their respective food categories, meaning both acquirees have a significant brand presence and economies of scale in manufacturing and distribution in the New Zealand and Australian markets.

However, the favorable traits of this deal aren't enough to overcome the dilemma of acquisitions. Despite all the favorable attributes there are to a corporate acquisition, a favorable price, and a strong market leader, in 2016 and onwards, URC's return on capital and margin would decline.

This underperformance suggests that its rationale to leverage its existing ASEAN distribution to bolster these new acquisitions' growth plan didn't pan out as expected. Additionally, analysts have attributed this failure to the changing consumer preference from salty and sweet snacks to healthy ones and the maturity of the Oceania market.

URC would later consolidate its New Zealand and Australia segments into one operating segment and enter a joint venture with Intersnack Group by selling 40% of the consolidated segment. And eventually would fully divest it in 2021 for a loss of around ₱16B out of the ₱47.7B paid on both acquisitions. This does not include the opportunity cost of capital paid on the transaction, which, based on my estimate, will be around ₱7.55B if it were invested in the long-term Philippine government bond yielding around 4% around that time. In summary, the total loss of this venture is around ₱23.55B.

URC would later use the proceeds from the divestiture of its Oceania segment to acquire Munchy for a sum of ₱22.8B, which is a biscuits market leader in Malaysia. The rationale of the acquisition isn't that much different from that of Griffin Foods and Snackbrands. Munchy under URC would gain access to the company's extensive ASEAN distribution, manufacturing, research, and development capabilities outside of its home market. Additionally, the acquisition has reinforced the company's strategy to refocus on the ASEAN region, which they believe is a more attractive market due to its high-growth potential. Since refocusing on the ASEAN region, URC's financial performance has improved.

I believe that URC is refocusing on the ASEAN region and reinforcing that commitment by acquiring Munchy will deliver superb growth and shareholder value creation. Unlike the URC's first high-sticker-price acquisition involving Griffin Foods and Snackbrands, Munchy is a more focused acquisition. The Griffin and Snackbrands acquisition involves multiple consumer food categories (salty snacks, sweets, and crackers), while Munchy only involves one line of food category (biscuits). Munchy's focus on the biscuit food category will allow the URC management to put a significant amount of attention on making sure that their strategy for the Munchy brand, or the reason why they acquired it in the first place, becomes a success. The principle of why I like companies that refocus their business is that management's attention is a scarce resource. It is better spent on ventures where the company has a better chance of winning, which usually means where they are a leader in or where their core capabilities currently are. Additionally, the Munchy acquisition strengthens URC's biscuit portfolio, especially in the Philippines, where they are currently 3rd place in market share.

URC intends to play in the higher price segment of the Philippine biscuit market, which, I believe, makes sense as there is a tendency of premiumization as consumer purchasing power improves. This also presents a margin expansion opportunity for the company as they could charge a significant premium for this segment.

After 2 years of integrating Munchy into URC, the management believes that it's now ready to aggressively push the brand into other countries, outside of Malaysia. I believe this focus and more coherent approach this time by URC has a better chance of being a success.

Suboptimal Capital Structure

One of the assessments that an investor needs to consider when investing in a company, especially in Philippine equities, is: Does its capital structure make sense considering the circumstances of the business? The principle that I use is: 'If demand is predictable and stable, capitalize the balance sheet with more debt with adequate equity, and if demand is cyclical or unpredictable, then maintain a strong balance sheet by maintaining a high amount of equity and minimal debt to protect the shareholders against market downturns. There is some sort of subjectivity in determining the appropriate mix between equity and debt that a company should have.
